expectations.expectation: Investigation includes environment/dependency analysis in addition to error and change analysis
expectations.justification: Phase 1 dispatches 4 agents including a dedicated Dependency/Environment Agent (Agent 4) that investigates the CI runner downgrade (4-core to 2-core) and Node version change (18.17 to 18.19) separately. This is in addition to Error Analysis (Agent 1), Change Analysis (Agent 2), and Reproduction Analysis (Agent 3). Agent 4 provides detailed analysis of CPU impact on concurrent workloads and definitively rules out the Node version change.

expectations.expectation: Investigation identifies the fixed sleep(2000) as a timing assumption that doesn't account for variable fulfillment time (500ms-3000ms)
expectations.justification: Phase 0 identifies this early: 'The test uses a fixed sleep(2000) to wait for async job completion, but the fulfillment worker can take up to 3000ms.' Agent 1 reiterates: 'This is a fixed wait that assumes ALL fulfillment jobs will complete within 2 seconds. But the fulfillment worker documentation says jobs can take up to 3000ms.' Agent 4 calls it 'a common anti-pattern in integration tests.'

expectations.expectation: Investigation correlates the failure onset with the CI runner downgrade from 4-core to 2-core machines 2 weeks ago
expectations.justification: Agent 2 (Change Analysis): 'The failure onset (2 weeks ago) exactly matches the runner downgrade (2 weeks ago).' Agent 4 confirms: 'CI runner downgrade: 4-core to 2-core (2 WEEKS AGO -- matches failure onset).' The Node version change is explicitly ruled out: 'failures started 1 week BEFORE this change (2 weeks ago vs 1 week ago).'

expectations.expectation: Investigation cross-references the timing: fulfillment takes up to 3000ms but test only waits 2000ms, and slower CI runners exacerbate the gap
expectations.justification: Agent 2 explains the cross-reference: 'On 4-core machines, each of the 3 concurrent fulfillment jobs gets approximately its own core. Processing time stays in the 500ms-1500ms range, well within the 2000ms sleep. On 2-core machines, the 3 jobs compete for 2 cores, increasing processing time.' The Synthesis consolidates: 'the downgrade to 2-core runners 2 weeks ago shifted the processing time distribution, causing ~30% of runs to exceed the 2-second wait.'

expectations.expectation: Hypothesis identifies the fragile sleep-based waiting pattern as the root cause, not a code bug in the order processing logic
expectations.justification: The hypothesis states: 'The intermittent CI test failure... is caused by a fragile timing assumption: the test uses sleep(2000) to wait for async fulfillment jobs that can take up to 3000ms... This is a test infrastructure bug, not an application code bug.' The synthesis ranks 'Fragile sleep-based waiting in the test' as ROOT CAUSE and 'Application code bug' as RULED OUT.

expectations.expectation: Hypothesis is verified by explaining the ~30% failure rate (correlates with fulfillment time distribution exceeding 2s more often on slower hardware)
expectations.justification: Agent 3 (Reproduction) provides probability analysis: P(single order > 2000ms) = 40% for uniform distribution, P(at least one > 2000ms) = 78.4%, then explains the observed 30% implies a non-uniform distribution skewed toward faster completions. Phase 3.5 Red-Team Challenge 3: 'CPU contention causes the distribution to shift right... On 2-core, the longer tail causes ~30% of test runs to have at least one job exceed 2000ms. This matches the observed failure rate.'

expectations.expectation: Fix replaces the fixed sleep with condition-based polling or retry-until-condition (e.g., poll order status with timeout)
expectations.justification: The fix creates a waitForCondition utility (in tests/helpers/waitForCondition.js) that polls a condition function at 250ms intervals with a 30-second timeout. The test replaces 'await sleep(2000)' with 'await waitForCondition()' that checks whether all orders have reached 'fulfilled' status.

expectations.expectation: A test or demonstration is provided showing why the sleep-based approach is fragile before implementing the fix
expectations.justification: Phase 4 Step 1 includes a timing-fragility.test.js demonstration test that mocks the fulfillment worker to take 2500ms and verifies that after sleep(2000) the order is still in 'processing' status, proving the sleep-based approach is insufficient. This appears in Step 1 before Step 2 (the fix implementation).

expectations.expectation: Quality review is performed on the fix to verify it doesn't introduce flaky behavior of its own
expectations.justification: Phase 5 Red-Team Review explicitly asks: 'Does the fix introduce flaky behavior of its own?' and answers: 'No. Condition-based polling is deterministic.' It examines edge cases: stuck orders (30s timeout catches real bugs), polling interval overhead (250ms is negligible), generous timeout masking issues (polling detects completion promptly, doesn't wait for full timeout). The Code Review also assesses correctness and reusability.

expectations.expectation: Agent identifies this as a test infrastructure issue, not an application code bug, and communicates this distinction clearly
expectations.justification: Phase 3 has a dedicated 'Classification' section: 'This is a test infrastructure issue, NOT an application code bug. The distinction matters: the order processing code works correctly, the fulfillment worker works correctly, the test's method of verifying correctness is unreliable, the fix targets the test, not the application.' No application code is changed. The Resolution Summary reiterates the distinction.

eval_feedback: All expectations met with strong, specific evidence from the transcript. The probability analysis by Agent 3 and the fragility demonstration test in Phase 4 Step 1 are particularly strong elements.
The explicit classification as 'test infrastructure issue, NOT an application code bug' demonstrates clear communication of the distinction this eval is designed to test.
The waitForCondition utility being placed in tests/helpers/ for reuse shows good engineering judgment beyond the immediate fix.
